stem: A group homomorphism \( \varphi: G \to H \) satisfies \( |G| = [[g]] \) and \( |\ker\varphi| = [[k]] \). Using the First Isomorphism Theorem \( G/\ker\varphi \cong \operatorname{im}\varphi \), find \( |\operatorname{im}\varphi| \).
solution: By the First Isomorphism Theorem, \( G/\ker\varphi \cong \operatorname{im}\varphi \), so \( |\operatorname{im}\varphi| = |G|/|\ker\varphi| = [[g]]/[[k]] = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( |G| \) itself, forgetting to quotient by the kernel at all.
This is \( |\ker\varphi| \), confusing the kernel's size with the image's size.
This subtracts the kernel order from \( |G| \) instead of dividing.
This adds the kernel order to \( |G| \) instead of dividing.
This is off by one above the correct image order.
This is off by one below the correct image order.

stem: Let \( G = \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) (integers mod \([[n]]\) under addition) and define \( \varphi: G \to G \) by \( \varphi(x) = [[a]]x \bmod [[n]] \), a group homomorphism. Using the First Isomorphism Theorem, find \( |\operatorname{im}\varphi| \).
solution: The kernel is \( \{x : [[a]]x \equiv 0 \pmod{[[n]]}\} \), which has \( \gcd([[n]],[[a]]) \) elements. By the First Isomorphism Theorem, \( |\operatorname{im}\varphi| = |G|/|\ker\varphi| = [[n]]/\gcd([[n]],[[a]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( |G| \) itself, as if \( \varphi \) were always injective.
This is \( |\ker\varphi| = \gcd([[n]],[[a]]) \), the wrong side of the theorem.
This mistakes the multiplier \( [[a]] \) itself for the image size.
This subtracts the kernel order from \( n \) instead of dividing.
This is off by one above the correct image order.
This is off by one below the correct image order.

stem: Let \( G = (\mathbb{Z}/[[p]]\mathbb{Z})^{\times} \), the group of units mod \([[p]]\) under multiplication (\([[p]]\) prime, so \( |G| = \varphi([[p]]) = [[p]]-1 \)). Define \( \phi: G \to G \) by \( \phi(x) = x^{[[k]]} \bmod [[p]] \), a group homomorphism. Using the First Isomorphism Theorem, find \( |\operatorname{im}\phi| \).
solution: Since \( G \) is cyclic of order \( \varphi([[p]]) = [[p]]-1 \), the kernel of \( x \mapsto x^{[[k]]} \) has order \( \gcd([[p]]-1,[[k]]) \). By the First Isomorphism Theorem, \( |\operatorname{im}\phi| = |G|/|\ker\phi| = ([[p]]-1)/\gcd([[p]]-1,[[k]]) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is \( |G| \) itself, forgetting the kernel is generally nontrivial.
This is \( |\ker\phi| \), the wrong side of the isomorphism theorem.
This mistakes the exponent \( [[k]] \) itself for the image size.
This subtracts \( [[k]] \) from \( [[p]] \), an unrelated combination.
This is off by one above the correct image order.
This is off by one below the correct image order.

stem: The sign homomorphism \( \operatorname{sgn}: S_{[[n]]} \to \{1,-1\} \) sends a permutation to \(+1\) if it is even and \(-1\) if it is odd. Given \( |S_{[[n]]}| = [[n]]! \) and \( |\operatorname{im}(\operatorname{sgn})| = 2 \), use the First Isomorphism Theorem to find \( |\ker(\operatorname{sgn})| \) (the order of the alternating group \( A_{[[n]]} \)).
distractor_rationales: This is \( |S_{[[n]]}| \) itself, forgetting to quotient by the image size.
This is \( |\operatorname{im}(\operatorname{sgn})| \), the wrong side of the theorem.
This uses \( (n-1)! \), an unrelated factorial.
This divides by 4 instead of by the actual image order of 2.
This is off by one above the correct kernel order.
This is off by one below the correct kernel order.

stem: Let \( G = \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \times \mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \), so \( |G| = [[m]]\cdot[[n]] \). Define \( \pi: G \to \mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]} \) by \( \pi(a,b) = a \), the projection homomorphism, whose kernel is \( \{0\}\times\mathbb{Z}_{[[n]]} \) of order \([[n]]\). Using the First Isomorphism Theorem, find \( |\operatorname{im}\pi| \).
solution: By the First Isomorphism Theorem, \( |\operatorname{im}\pi| = |G|/|\ker\pi| = ([[m]]\cdot[[n]])/[[n]] = [[answer]] \), matching \( |\mathbb{Z}_{[[m]]}| \) as expected.
distractor_rationales: This is \( |\ker\pi| \), the order of the second factor, not the image.
This is \( |G| \) itself, forgetting to quotient by the kernel.
This subtracts the two factor orders instead of using the quotient.
This adds the two factor orders instead of using the quotient.
This is off by one above the correct image order.
This is off by one below the correct image order.
